author: @jasonlange780
text: I’ve had to do this a couple of times but there’s no need to remove the whole carriage. There’s three bolts that remove just the head from the carriage assembly and it makes it a whole lot easier reassembling two bolts that go through the ribbon cable holder on the top of, the printhead and one in about the middle between the wheels…. It saves fluffing about what the carriage because there’s no need unless there’s damage or you replacing the carriage wheels….

author: @CuttinInIdaho
text:
I looked at how this hotend is designed, it needs to be all metal.  Yeah, I have been where you are.   I caught mine a lot sooner, but the problem is it builds up under the sock before you are any wiser.  

There are 3 m3 screws on the back so you don't have to take the wheels off.  2 are holding the cable mount, and one is by the bottom wheels in between them.  All run through the PCB on the back of the assembly.  Then take the 2 fan shroud m3's out which hold the fans to the front of the carrier.   You have to remove all of these screws no matter what, so you may as well not take the wheels off.

Reason?  That PTFE tube must be perfectly cut in order to prevent problems like this.  If it isn't, you won't know until you are tearing it apart, again, and unclogging it.   There is also no way to check the tube to make sure there is no play in it when you reassemble, so you are shooting in the dark.  The leak comes from the threads of the heat break and the nozzle.

Elegoo sends a spare piece cut longer than it needs to be on purpose...that way the fault is on you because you have to trim it.  No slop is acceptable with a full PTFE lined hotend...none.  My failure was from the factory, so no modifications of any kind.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: I would suggest using the hot air gun from an SMD rework station instead of the soldering iron. It should make it easier to warm the PLA just enough to pull it off without risking any damage to the wiring and plastic parts. I'm guessing that somewhere between 120 and 150C the PLA would already be soft enough to carve, or pull all at once?

author: @stevennope8864
text: I've seen in the comments about heating it up via the menu, that's what I just recently did to clean up a fail on my nep4. It makes it easy to get the screws out of the nozzle block (noob not sure of terminology), just push in the Allen key and rotate till it lines up. The melted plastic just pushes out. Also, I soaked it in a glass of acetone to clean it up nice. It made the PLA brittle. Don't know if that was the right thing to do or not, but figured the only thing it could damage was the wire insulation and I have liquid electrical tape if needed. It works fine now.

replies.author: @stevennope8864
replies.text: Also, cup of steaming hot water from a microwave might be enough to soften up the blob. I tried stirring my coffee with a failed print, just to see if I could use it for something, then the print warped and began to fall into the cup. Wish I remembered that before I tried the acetone.

text: A simple trick to make sure you never plug the wires in the wrong place is to have a set of permanent markers of 10 different colors. Also I use a pair of fine point electronic snips for controllable cutting of plastic. Many ways to skin a ......

author: @robson668
text:
First thing to try is to heat the hotend up and try pulling the blob off with pliers on the opposite side of the wires first.
This will obviously only work if thermistor and heating cartridge wires are still intact :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a god suggestion mate if the plastic isn't too thick - other wise I think you will just cause more damage

replies.author: @NiteLynr
replies.text:
My first (of three) Neptune 3s had a catastrophic failure of the 50's B-movie level. I reached out to Elegoo at the time asking if they had replacement parts available and they guided me through fixing it without removing the hotend. Basically, as long as the wiring isn't damaged, use the menu to preheat it and just peel the blob off with pliers. Worked an absolute treat and I was up and running in no time. I appreciate from previous printer clogs that sometimes the wiring gets damaged and this is not an option but I would certainly try this option as a first attempt.
(BTW, said blob is sitting on my 'shelf of shame' where I keep all my blobs as reminders.)

author: @corydwagner
text:
I print most of my models overnight. It makes it seem more like magic when you come down in the morning and see something where there was nothing.

I do usually make sure the first couple of layers go down okay before heading to bed.

I have had only one clog, and it was nowhere as near as bad as yours. Most of my fails have resulted in spaghetti everywhere if a layer shifts or a support falls over.
